does: This paper introduces Alignment from Demonstrations (AfD), a method for aligning large language models using high-quality demonstration data rather than preference annotations. The authors formalize AfD as a sequential decision-making problem and propose divergence minimization objectives inspired by both forward and inverse reinforcement learning. They develop a computationally efficient algorithm that learns a reward model tailored to the AfD setting and uses it to guide language model alignment. The approach is validated on harmlessness and helpfulness tasks, where it achieves strong empirical performance while remaining simpler than existing preference-based methods.
connects: This work builds on established lines of research in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for LLM alignment, but addresses key limitations of preference-based approaches by drawing from inverse reinforcement learning theory. It extends prior work on learning from demonstrations and reward learning by adapting these techniques to the language model alignment domain. The paper connects to theoretical frameworks for understanding mass-covering versus mode-seeking behavior in imitation learning and inverse RL, synthesizing insights from both forward RL and inverse RL to motivate the divergence minimization objectives. It also responds to practical concerns in existing alignment pipelines around noisy preference labels, annotation costs, and privacy issues inherent in human feedback collection.
enables: This work opens pathways for more efficient and privacy-preserving LLM alignment by establishing that demonstration data alone can guide learning without explicit preference annotations. The theoretical analysis of mass-covering and mode-seeking behaviors provides grounding for future algorithm design choices in imitation learning and reward learning. The efficient reward model learning approach could enable scaling alignment techniques to larger models and datasets with reduced computational overhead. The framework also suggests opportunities for combining demonstration-based alignment with other signals or learning paradigms, potentially leading to hybrid approaches that leverage multiple data sources for more robust alignment while maintaining practical computational and privacy benefits over current preference-based methods.
